Saudi Arabia Starts to Shift Crude Exports to Red Sea as Hormuz Disruption Mounts -- Commodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

--Brent crude oil is up 2.7% to $83.57 a barrel.

--European benchmark gas is up 6.2% at 51.80 euros a megawatt hour.

--Gold futures are up 0.8% to $5,174.10 a troy ounce.

--LME three-month copper futures are down 1.6% to $12,844.50 a metric ton.


TOP STORY:

Saudi Arabia Starts to Shift Crude Exports to Red Sea as Hormuz Disruption Mounts

Saudi Arabia is starting to shift crude exports to its Red Sea hub at Yanbu as disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz constrain Gulf shipments.

According to Kpler vessel-tracking data, about 10 million barrels of crude were loaded from the Red Sea's Al-Muajjiz terminal in the first four days of March, implying a daily pace of roughly 2.5 million barrels.


OTHER STORIES:

Strait of Hormuz: The Oil Bottleneck Threatening the Global Economy

President Trump's promise to protect the Strait of Hormuz with naval escorts and provide government-backed marine insurance underscores the urgent need to restore flows of energy from the Middle East before soaring prices rip through the world economy.

As of Wednesday, day five of the war on Iran, several thousand ships were stuck inside and outside the Persian Gulf, trapping roughly a fifth of the oil and liquefied natural gas the world consumes each day. The blockage is cascading through the region's industry as storage tanks fill up with oil that can't set sail, forcing producers to slash output.

--

Venezuelan Oil Cargoes Are Surging at an Opportune Time

The global oil market is reeling after U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran shut the world's biggest oil-shipping artery. But tankers are ferrying more crude from an unlikely source: Venezuela.

U.S. imports of Venezuelan oil have climbed to their highest level in more than a year, just months after President Trump implemented an oil blockade that crippled the shadow fleet transporting the Latin American country's heavy crude.

--

Maple Leaf Foods 4Q Profit, Revenue Rise

Maple Leaf Foods logged higher profit as stronger pricing and higher sales in prepared foods and poultry drove revenue growth.

The Canadian consumer-packaged meats and food production company on Thursday reported a higher net income of 391.2 million Canadian dollars ($286.7 million), or C$3.14 a share, up from C$53.5 million, or C$0.43 a share, in the comparable quarter a year ago.


MARKET TALKS:

Oil Futures Rise As Persian Gulf Conflict Continues -- Market Talk

0905 ET - Crude futures extend their rally with the Middle East conflict in its sixth day, disrupting shipping and energy infrastructure. Uncertainty surrounding the conflict's duration is keeping upward pressure "firmly in place," Frank Walbaum of Naga says. He notes Iraq's shutting in production for lack of storage, with traders "actively pricing in the risk of broader forced shutdowns if the disruptions continue." While U.S. plans to support tanker insurance and deploy naval escorts could limit spikes, "any meaningful market pullbacks will likely depend on clear signs of geopolitical de-escalation or the sustained reopening of commercial tanker flows." Brent rises 2.4% to $83.36 a barrel and WTI gains 3% to $76.93.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

Gold Higher as Investors Seek Safety -- Market Talk

1215 GMT - Gold prices rise, pushing closer to $5,200 a troy ounce as the Middle East conflict drags on, lifting demand for safer assets. New York futures are up 0.8% to $5,175.20 an ounce. "The inflationary impact of the Middle East conflict, via sharply higher energy prices, could reinforce expectations of higher interest rates for longer--a headwind for nonyielding assets such as gold," analysts at ING say. "However, elevated geopolitical uncertainty continues to support a risk premium, helping to underpin prices despite the challenging rates backdrop." Meanwhile, silver futures rise 1.3% to $84.31 an ounce. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Palm Oil Ended Higher, Tracking Higher Crude Oil Prices -- Market Talk

1040 GMT - Palm oil ended higher, tracking higher crude oil prices amid escalating tensions in the Middle East, according to David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz continues to support oil prices. Ng sees prices of crude palm oil supported at 4,150 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,300 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for May delivery rose 26 ringit to 4,205 ringgit a ton. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

--

Qatar LNG Exports Could Resume Late March But Strait of Hormuz Safety Crucial, Kpler Says -- Market Talk

0925 GMT - Qatar's liquefied natural gas exports could resume toward the end of March following the shutdown of the key hub Ras Laffan, but much depends on safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz, says Laura Page from Kpler. "We think these exports will likely start off as exports from the storage tanks while the plant restarts production," the energy analyst says. "The restart process could take about one to two weeks." Page says March exports will be sharply impacted by the shutdown, with knock-on effects likely extending into April. Kpler currently forecasts Middle Eastern LNG exports in March to drop to around 2.4 million metric tons from prior estimates of 8.2 million tons. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

European Gas Prices Extend Gains on LNG Supply Fears -- Market Talk

0828 GMT - European natural-gas prices extend gains in early trading after top LNG exporter Qatar halted production at a key plant and declared force majeure on shipments, jolting global gas markets. "The disruption to LNG supplies comes at a particularly vulnerable time. Inventories are low, particularly in Europe, as the heating season for Asia and Europe nears its end," analysts at ANZ say. "As a result, restocking will become increasingly difficult for consumers over the coming months, given limited alternative sources." The benchmark Dutch TTF contract climbs 10.5% to 53.87 a megawatt-hour, with year-to-date gains of 90%. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Dollar Strength Could Continue Weighing on Gold -- Market Talk

0634 GMT - The dollar's strength could continue weighing on gold prices, according to Phillip Nova's Priyanka Sachdeva. The unexpected surge in the U.S. currency likely played a role in some profit-taking in gold, despite the widening Middle East conflict, the analyst says in a note. Inflation fears have revived, along with the possibility of fewer Federal Reserve rate cuts this year, given higher energy prices. Nonetheless, gold is still hovering around 18% year-to-date gains, despite retreating from $5,400. Investors should take into account the dollar's rise, the pace of gold-buying by central banks and any escalation in the Iran conflict when positioning in gold, she adds. Spot gold is flat at $5,141.36 a troy ounce. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)


Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-05-26 0948ET

Cartels can only be defeated with military power, White House's Miller says
data.description:
MIAMI, March 5 (Reuters) - Cartels operating in the Western Hemisphere can only be defeated with military power, White House Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen Miller said on Thursday.

"We have learned after decades of effort is that there is not a criminal justice solution to the cartel problem," Miller told Latin American defense leaders gathered at the U.S. Southern Command headquarters for a conference.

(Reporting by Phil Stewart in Florida; Writing by Ryan Patrick Jones; Editing by Susan Heavey)

data.title: TSX opens lower as miners fall; investors weigh Middle East crisis
data.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - Canada's main stock index opened lower on Thursday, dragged down by mining shares as gold prices declined, while investors practiced caution amid the ongoing Middle East conflict that entered its sixth day. 

At 9:32 a.m. ET, the S&P/TSX composite index was down 1% at 33,627.88 points.

(; Editing by Shreya Biswas)
